templates.text: '{echoText}.' You speak it aloud. The echo returns in a voice that is not yours.
The walls absorb '{echoText}'. When you press your palm to the stone, you feel it — the word, trapped inside, still vibrating.
'{echoText}' — the dungeon tries to pronounce it. Fails. Tries again. The second attempt is closer.
You whisper '{echoText}'. Three rooms away, something responds.
Someone carried '{echoText}' into the dungeon long before you. It stayed behind when they left — or when they didn't leave.
This language lives in the stone now. '{echoText}' — one more word added to the dungeon's vocabulary. It learns from every seeker.
'{echoText}.' The word is older than its translation. Down here, the older version is what survives.
The word '{echoText}' appears on the wall — not carved, not painted. Simply present. As if the stone learned it from you.
The dungeon knows you said '{echoText}'. It has been writing it down somewhere in the lower levels since the moment you spoke.
This corridor has heard '{echoText}' before. The acoustics know the word — they shape it differently, more carefully, than any other sound you've made.
'{echoText}' — the word carries weight in different mouths. In yours, right now, it is heavier than expected.
'{echoText}.' Filed. The dungeon does not discriminate between languages — it absorbs all of them equally, which is to say: completely.
